comments.body:
[G3 child-coverage — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with this child (S2 Content-addressed snapshot cache + background janitor)]

Audited this child's contribution to the parent's acceptance/success criteria; every absence cross-checked against the WHOLE sibling roster (S1 6d56, S3 fa49, S4 30b3, S5 b7fa, S6 1ba5). This child is the cache/janitor slice; the union of children covers every parent criterion. No uncovered parent criterion. Empty G3 findings list.

4-bucket coverage map (parent AC -> covering child/children; this child's role called out):
- Parent AC1 (no attested gate reads the working tree; all read a faithful snapshot materialized at the pinned SHA) — FULLY covered by S1 (6d56, materialize core: faithful tree + atomic population) + S3 (fa49, gate re-rooting onto the snapshot). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child: it caches the materialized entry but does not own materialization (its Scope: "Out: materialization itself (S1)"). Not an absence.
- Parent AC2 (signed verdict records verified_at_sha + reproducible over identical content) — FULLY covered by S4 (30b3, record+sign+in-toto) with the immutable-SHA basis from S1. This child reinforces reproducibility by making the SHA a content-addressed, no-staleness cache key, but does not sign (its Scope: "Out: verdict memoization"). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child. Not an absence (S4 covers signing).
- Parent AC3 (local mode reads in-place checkout AND cannot produce a signed verdict) — covered by S3 (in-place read) + S4 (signing bar). This child claims nothing here (local mode bypasses the cache: "no materialization, no cache" per S3). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child. Not an absence.
- Parent AC4 (concurrent gates on same/different SHAs run without git-lock contention or cross-op corruption; no worktrees; atomic population; delete-on-last-close) — FULLY covered, SPLIT cleanly: THIS child is the PRIMARY OWNER of the single-flight + delete-on-last-close half (its AC1: two concurrent gates on the same SHA trigger exactly one materialization, the second reads the shared entry; its AC2: evicting an entry while a reader holds it open does not disrupt the in-flight read, new lookups cleanly miss and re-materialize; its AC6: no PID/heartbeat lease, liveness rests on fds/flock). S1 owns the atomic-population + no-worktree + no index.lock/config.lock-contention half (S1 AC3/AC6). No double-implementation; no gap.
- Parent AC5 (snapshot cache + janitor reclaim disk under pressure without disrupting in-flight reads) — FULLY covered, with THIS child as the SOLE OWNER and heart of the slice: its AC3 (janitor reclaims under a free-space watermark using LRU by touch-on-read mtime NOT atime, skipping the grace window, off the hot path), its AC4 (eviction is rename-to-trash then rmtree; no in-place recursive delete of a live entry), its AC5 (a second process cannot run a GC pass concurrently — exclusive gc/lock; population remains lock-free), and its AC2 (eviction under an open fd does not disrupt the in-flight read — delete-on-last-close) deliver "reclaim disk under pressure WITHOUT disrupting in-flight reads" in full. Clean.
- Parent AC6 (private-repo fetch failure surfaces a descriptive, actionable credential error) — FULLY covered by S1 (raises the actionable error; its AC5) + S6 (1ba5, documents message + remedy + fail-closed/local-still-runs; its AC2). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child; claims none (fetch is S1's coalesced step). Not an absence.
- Parent AC7 (portable: no external tar; no hardcoded /tmp; flock+mkdir fallback) — FULLY covered by S1 (implementation: pure-Python extraction, gettempdir/mkdtemp, fcntl.flock + atomic-mkdir fallback; its AC4) + S6 (docs: REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR + EFS/NFS flock caveat; its AC5). This child's cross-process pieces (flock(LOCK_EX) on locks/<sha>, exclusive flock on gc/lock, POSIX delete-on-last-close) ride on the same portability seam S1 establishes but do not re-own the tar/tmp/flock-fallback sub-clauses. STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child. Not an absence.
- Parent AC8 (all six child stories closed; make lint + full test suite green) — STRUCTURAL/meta epic-completion criterion; covered by the existence of all six children (this child is story #2 of 6 in the roster) + each child's own green-bar obligation. Not an absence at this child.

Success-criteria narrative ("every code-reading gate verifies a client-specified, immutable, reproducible snapshot ... never the server's mutable checkout — SAFELY UNDER CONCURRENT USE by a distributed team; signed verdicts reproducible and bound to verified_at_sha; unsigned local preserves verify-before-push") decomposes onto AC1-AC5/AC7. The "safely under concurrent use" clause — specifically reclaiming disk without corrupting concurrent readers — is precisely this child's core deliverable (its AC1-AC5), atop the immutable-SHA cache key (no staleness) that makes "reproducible snapshot" hold across cache hits. The "signed/reproducible/verified_at_sha" and "unsigned local" clauses are carried by S4/S3. No uncovered obligation introduced.

Note: this child's "What" explicitly delegates to named consumers — materialization to S1 ("populated via S1's atomic dir-rename"; "Out: materialization itself (S1)") and verdict memoization deferred (fast-follow, also Out of parent scope). Each delegated obligation is claimed by a named sibling (S1) or out of epic scope — covered-by-named-consumer, not absences.

Note (scope, not a G3 finding): the existing G4 review on sibling S4 (30b3) flags a seam/dependency-gap around routing the pinned-SHA snapshot handle into plan_review/attest.py's hashing path between S3 and S4. That is a consistency (G4) concern about WHO threads the handle — not a G3 union-coverage gap — and is unrelated to this cache/janitor child; the corresponding parent obligation (AC1/AC2/ref-coherence) remains CLAIMED by children. G3 union-coverage of the parent criteria is complete.

Conclusion: this child cleanly covers the cache/janitor slice (content-addressed cache, single-flight, delete-on-last-close reader safety, free-space-watermark LRU reclamation, rename-to-trash eviction, GC interlock, self-heal) of the parent criteria — it is the sole owner of parent AC5 and a co-owner of the concurrency half of AC4; no parent acceptance/success criterion is left uncovered by the roster. No G3 finding for this pairing.

comments.body: PLAN-REVIEW TRIAGE (verdict PASS; 10 advisories). Accepted, to fold into ACs at implementation: (1) make the tuning thresholds CONFIGURABLE and state defaults — free-space watermark %, mtime grace window, max-age cutoff, integrity-reverify period; (2) add covering ACs for the max-age cold trim, the startup sweep (rmtree tmp/*,trash/*), and the incrementally-maintained byte total; (3) byte-total updates must be atomic/owned so concurrent lock-free populate vs under-gc/lock evict cannot drift (TOCTOU); (4) define trash straggler ownership if the janitor crashes after rename-to-trash but before rmtree, between startup sweeps; (5) write an ADR capturing the delete-on-last-close + flock-interlock + content-addressed-layout design AND the rejected PID+heartbeat lease (spike outcome). None judged invalid.

[G4 child-consistency — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with THIS child (S2 70c7: Content-addressed snapshot cache + background janitor)]

Ran the 7 cross-child interaction modes for this cache/janitor child against the WHOLE sibling roster (S1 6d56 materialize core, S3 fa49 gate threading, S4 30b3 attested signing, S5 b7fa CLI/MCP surfacing, S6 1ba5 docs). All 7 modes are clean under the high-confidence / benign-reading filter. EMPTY G4 findings list for this pairing.

Mode-by-mode:

1. Implicit shared state — CLEAN. This child OWNS the on-disk cache layout (`<cache>/<sha>/`, `tmp/`, `trash/`, `locks/<sha>`, `gc/lock`) and the running byte-total; it CONSUMES S1's atomic dir-rename primitive ("populated via S1's atomic dir-rename"). The producer/owner split at the cache root is mutually named and consistent: S1 builds into `<root>/tmp/<uuid>/` and renames into the cache path, and S1 AC3 states "a crashed materialization leaves only a `tmp/` dir, cleaned on startup"; THIS child's AC5 owns that cleanup ("Startup sweep clears `tmp/*` and `trash/*` and reconciles the byte total via one full walk"). S1 Scope "Out: the cache/janitor (S2)" ↔ S2 Scope "Out: materialization itself (S1)". No shared-state value is produced-without-consumer or consumed-without-producer; the tmp/ residue S1 leaves is precisely the residue this child sweeps. Consistent.

2. Conflicting assumptions — CLEAN. This child is purely the ATTESTED-path cache; its text makes no local-mode claim, which is consistent with S3's "Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (no materialization, no cache)" — local bypasses the cache entirely, so there is no contradicting assumption about whether local touches it. ADR ownership agrees across the roster: this child's AC13 owns the cache-architecture + rejected-PID+heartbeat-lease ADR, and S3 Scope explicitly defers to it ("the architecture ADR (authored in S2, cross-referenced here)"). S4's "ADR 0002" is the distinct pre-existing plan-review claim-gate ADR, not this child's cache ADR — no collision. The delete-on-last-close / kernel-guarantee posture (this child) and S1's faithful-snapshot content agree on a single immutable-SHA cache key (no staleness). No contradiction.

3. Dependency gap — CLEAN. This child's reader-safety model REQUIRES a consumer-side complement: readers must "open() files up front" and "treat ENOENT/EIO/short-read as miss → re-materialize". That complement is owned by a named sibling — S3 AC6 ("a gate tolerates an absent/being-GC'd snapshot dir by re-materializing (ENOENT → miss), never reading a partial tree"). The atomic-population primitive this child depends on is produced by S1 (present, claims it). The configurable tunables this child introduces (watermark, grace window, max-age cutoff, reverify period) are documented by S6 (its disk-cap AC). No unowned upstream or downstream on this child's surface. (The standing S3↔S4 attest.py snapshot-handle routing seam is downstream of S1's resolved-SHA output and unrelated to this cache/janitor child.)

4. Scope overlap — CLEAN. Two boundaries, both clean: (a) the atomic dir-rename population sits at the S1↔S2 boundary but is split cleanly — S1 OWNS the population primitive, this child CONSUMES it for cache-entry creation and adds eviction (rename-to-trash → rmtree) on top; no double-implementation (S1 Scope "Out: the cache/janitor (S2)"; S2 Scope "Out: materialization itself (S1)"). This child's "single-flight per SHA" (coalescing materialization for a given SHA) is a DISTINCT layer from S1's "coalesced git fetch origin" (coalescing the lock-taking fetch). (b) The portability flock seam is OWNED by S1 (fcntl.flock + atomic-mkdir fallback); this child's cross-process `flock(LOCK_EX)` on `locks/<sha>` and exclusive `flock` on `gc/lock` RIDE that seam without re-owning the tar/tmp/flock-fallback sub-clauses. No overlap.

5. Ordering violation — CLEAN. S1 carries `blocks 70c7` (this child) — i.e. S1 → S2, coherent with the producer→consumer flow (the materialize core precedes the cache that populates from it). This child carries empty deps; parent AC8 only requires all-closed + green, so no encoded ordering is violated.

6. Consumer impact — CLEAN. This child's delete-on-last-close contract (readers open fds up front; ENOENT/EIO/short-read → miss → re-materialize) imposes a reader obligation on its sole downstream consumer S3, which adopts exactly that in its AC6. The single-flight shared-entry semantics (second concurrent caller reads the shared entry) impose no redefinition on any sibling. No downstream contract is broken by this child's surface.

7. Residual references — CLEAN. All cross-refs resolve: S1's atomic dir-rename (real producer in roster); the rejected PID+heartbeat lease (the spike outcome cited in the parent's Context/Design narrative); `shutil.disk_usage` (real stdlib); the configurable tunables → documented by S6 (its disk-cap/knobs AC). No dangling reference.

Note: the cache/janitor module (src/rebar/_engine) does not yet exist in the tree (pre-manifest epic), so the analysis is grounded in the ticket-spec wording, not shipped code.

Note (not a G4 finding on THIS pairing): the standing dependency-gap finding on sibling S4 (30b3) — routing the pinned-SHA snapshot handle into plan_review/attest.py's dependency_hashes()/_rehash()/claim-gate freshness re-check — is a code seam between S3 and S4; it does not touch this cache/janitor child and is out of this pairing's scope.

Conclusion: no high-confidence cross-child inconsistency between this cache/janitor child (S2) and the parent/siblings. Its content-addressed cache, single-flight, delete-on-last-close reader safety, free-space-watermark LRU reclamation, rename-to-trash eviction, gc/lock interlock, byte-total integrity, and ADR form clean producer/consumer splits with S1 (population → cache), S3 (delete-on-last-close → ENOENT-miss tolerance), and S6 (tunables → docs). Empty G4 findings list for this pairing.

comments.body:
## Completion Verification — PASS

Reviewed all six Acceptance Criteria against the implementation in `src/rebar/_snapshot/cache.py`, `src/rebar/_snapshot/__init__.py`, `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py`, and `tests/unit/test_snapshot_cache.py`.

---

### AC1 — Two concurrent gates requesting the same SHA trigger exactly ONE materialization (in-process single-flight)

**MET.**

`cache.py` maintains a `_sha_locks: dict[str, threading.Lock]` map (lines 55–65). `acquire()` takes `_sha_lock(sha)` before calling `materialize()` and re-checks `dest.is_dir()` after each lock acquisition (double-checked locking, lines 188–201). The test `test_in_process_single_flight` runs 4 threads, monkeypatches `_materialize_tree` with a counter, and asserts `len(builds) == 1`. A second test, `test_in_process_lock_collapses_without_flock`, pins the in-process lock in isolation (with `_interprocess_lock` replaced by `nullcontext`) and also asserts exactly one build.

---

### AC2 — Two PROCESSES racing the same SHA collapse to one materialization (`flock(LOCK_EX)`)

**MET.**

`acquire()` calls `_interprocess_lock(_sha_lock_path(root, sha))` (line 192, `cache.py`), which is `_interprocess_lock` from `repo_snapshot.py` (lines 214–250) — a `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` with an atomic-mkdir spin-lock fallback. The test `test_cross_process_single_flight` spawns 3 real subprocesses competing for the same SHA, each writing to a shared marker file before sleeping, and asserts `builds == 1` after all processes complete.

---

### AC3 — An entry removed while a reader holds it open does NOT disrupt the in-flight read (delete-on-last-close); a new lookup cleanly misses and re-materializes

**MET.**

`open_in_snapshot()` (lines 143–155, `cache.py`) opens a file and returns the fd; any `FileNotFoundError`/`OSError` on open raises `CacheMiss`. The test `test_open_reader_survives_eviction_then_relookup_misses` acquires an entry, opens a file fd, then does a janitor-style `os.rename(dest, trash)` + `shutil.rmtree(trash)`, reads the fd (asserts the content is still readable via POSIX delete-on-last-close), then calls `acquire()` again and asserts the entry is re-materialized clean.

---

### AC4 — `ENOENT`/read-error on a cache entry is treated as a miss and self-healed by re-materialization

**MET.**

`open_in_snapshot()` translates `FileNotFoundError → CacheMiss` and other `OSError → CacheMiss` (lines 151–155). The `acquire()` loop re-materializes when `dest.is_dir()` is false (miss path at lines 188–201). Tests cover both paths: `test_open_in_snapshot_raises_cache_miss_on_enoent` asserts `CacheMiss` on missing file; `test_deleted_entry_self_heals` does `shutil.rmtree(dest)` on an existing entry then calls `acquire()` again and asserts the entry is recreated.

---

### AC5 — Cache-hit recency is tracked by touch-on-read `mtime` (NOT `atime`); no PID/heartbeat lease exists

**MET.**

`touch_entry()` (lines 75–83) calls `os.utime(path, None)` — `None` updates both atime and mtime to "now" using the OS wall clock, but the design doc is explicit that mtime is the recency signal consumed by the janitor. The `acquire()` hot-path calls `touch_entry(dest)` on every cache hit (lines 183, 190–191, 195–196) and never writes any PID. The test `test_cache_hit_touches_mtime` sets the entry's mtime to `now - 1000`, calls `acquire()` again, and asserts `entry_mtime(dest) > past + 100`. The test `test_no_pid_or_heartbeat_lease_in_module` reads `cache.__file__` source text and asserts neither `"getpid("` nor `"kill("` appears; confirmed via tool search: no `getpid` hit in the module.

---

### AC6 — The byte-total increments atomically on populate (the value the janitor reconciles against)

**MET.**

`add_bytes()` (lines 109–125, `cache.py`) serializes under `_interprocess_lock(root / "locks" / "bytes.total.lock")`, reads the current total, adds `delta`, writes to a `.tmp` file, and atomically replaces with `os.replace()` — no TOCTOU. `acquire()` calls `add_bytes(entry_size(dest), root)` exactly once per real populate (line 200), inside the cross-process flock, so concurrent populates cannot double-count. Tests: `test_byte_total_increments_on_populate` checks monotone increments after two distinct SHAs; `test_byte_total_not_double_counted_on_cache_hit` calls `acquire()` twice on the same SHA and asserts the total does not increase on the second call; `test_add_bytes_atomic_under_concurrency` runs 50 concurrent `add_bytes(+1)` calls and asserts the final total equals 50 (no lost update).

---

### File-impact obligations

All three files listed in the ticket's `file_impact` are present and non-empty:
- `src/rebar/_snapshot/cache.py` — 201-line implementation
- `src/rebar/_snapshot/__init__.py` — exports all cache symbols (`CacheMiss`, `acquire`, `add_bytes`, `byte_total`, `entry_mtime`, `entry_size`, `open_in_snapshot`, `touch_entry`)
- `tests/unit/test_snapshot_cache.py` — 12 unit tests mapping to all 6 ACs

### Scope boundaries respected

- Eviction/janitor/disk reclamation: explicitly absent, correctly delegated to sibling S2b (b73c)
- Materialization: consumed via `repo_snapshot.materialize` (S1), not re-implemented

**Verdict: PASS. Every stated Acceptance Criterion is demonstrably implemented and covered by tests in the repository.**

compiled_state.description:
# Content-addressed snapshot cache (single-flight + reader-safety)

## Why

The same SHA is often requested by multiple concurrent gates; re-materializing identical
content per call is wasteful. An immutable SHA is a perfect cache key (no staleness), so
`<cache>/<sha>/` is content-addressed. This story builds the CACHE READ PATH only — safe
concurrent population + reads. RECLAMATION under disk pressure is the sibling janitor story
(it is "the hard part" and is split out to keep this a thin, provable slice).

## What

- SHA-keyed entries populated via S1's atomic dir-rename (consumes S1's output contract).
- Single-flight per SHA: in-process lock/future + optional cross-process `flock(LOCK_EX)` on
  `locks/<sha>` (a lost race is merely wasteful, never wrong — SHA=content).
- Reader safety via POSIX delete-on-last-close: readers `open()` files up front; an entry
  evicted (by the janitor) under an open fd stays readable; `ENOENT`/`EIO`/short-read ->
  treat as miss -> re-materialize.
- LRU recency signal: `mtime` TOUCHED-ON-READ on every cache hit (never `atime`; broken under
  relatime/noatime) — the janitor consumes this; no PID/heartbeat lease anywhere.
- Byte-total: increment atomically on populate (the janitor reconciles/decrements).

## Scope

- In: SHA-keyed layout, single-flight (in-process + cross-process flock), delete-on-last-close
  reads, touch-on-read mtime, populate-side byte-total increment, read-error self-heal.
- Out: eviction / janitor / disk reclamation (sibling janitor story); materialization (S1).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Two concurrent gates requesting the same SHA trigger exactly ONE materialization
      (in-process single-flight); the second reads the shared entry.
- [ ] Two PROCESSES racing the same SHA also collapse to one materialization (`flock(LOCK_EX)`).
- [ ] An entry removed (rename-away/unlink) while a reader holds it open does NOT disrupt the
      in-flight read (delete-on-last-close); a new lookup cleanly misses and re-materializes.
- [ ] `ENOENT`/read-error on a cache entry is treated as a miss and self-healed by re-materialization.
- [ ] Cache-hit recency is tracked by touch-on-read `mtime` (NOT `atime`); no PID/heartbeat lease exists.
- [ ] The byte-total increments atomically on populate (the value the janitor reconciles against).
